title: Filarial and Related Infections
authors: Philip J. Budge, Thomas B. Nutman

items.subtopic: Filarial and Related Infections | LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS
items.title: Understanding the Chronic Nature of Lymphatic Filariasis
items.content: Lymphatic filariasis, caused by W. bancrofti, B. malayi, or Brugia timori, is a chronic infection due to its long-term presence in lymphatics and nodes. Adult parasites can remain viable for over two decades.
keyPoints: Caused by W. bancrofti, B. malayi, or Brugia timori
Adult worms reside in lymphatics or lymph nodes
Can remain viable for more than 20 years
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · Filarial and Related Infections | LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS

items.subtopic: Filarial and Related Infections | LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS
items.title: Clinical Manifestations of Lymphatic Filariasis: Acute vs. Chronic Exposure
items.content: The clinical manifestations of lymphatic filariasis differ based on exposure duration. Natives in endemic areas experience chronic, less intense symptoms, while newly exposed individuals face more acute and severe presentations.
keyPoints: Chronic infection with long-term debilitating effects
Natives have lifelong exposure leading to milder symptoms
Newly exposed individuals exhibit more acute and intense symptoms
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · Filarial and Related Infections | LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS

items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology of Wuchereria bancrofti Infections
items.content: W. bancrofti, a filarial parasite affecting approximately 51 million people globally, is prevalent in tropical and subtropical regions including Asia, Africa, South America, and the Caribbean. The nocturnally periodic form dominates outside the Pacific Islands, where subperiodic forms are more common.
keyPoints: W. bancrofti affects an estimated 51 million people worldwide.
Nocturnally periodic microfilariae increase at night; subperiodic forms are present all day and peak in the afternoon.
Culex mosquitoes are urban vectors, while Anopheles or Aedes mosquitoes serve as rural vectors.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOLOGY

items.title: Pathologic Changes in Lymphatic Filariasis and Their Implications
items.content: Lymphatic filariasis pathology is primarily due to adult worms causing inflammatory damage, leading to lymphatic dilation, thickening of vessel walls, and compromised lymphatic function. The host's immune response exacerbates these changes.
keyPoints: Adult worms cause lymphatic dilation and wall thickening.
Inflammatory infiltration includes plasma cells, eosinophils, and macrophages.
Lymphedema results from damaged or incompetent lymph valves.
Granulomatous reactions and fibrosis occur post-worm death, leading to lymphatic obstruction.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOLOGY

items.title: Subclinical Manifestations of Lymphatic Filariasis
items.content: Most individuals infected with W. bancrofti or B. malayi exhibit subclinical disease, characterized by microscopic hematuria and/or proteinuria, dilated lymphatics, and scrotal lymphangiectasia in men.
keyPoints: Subclinical manifestations include microscopic hematuria and/or proteinuria.
Dilated (and tortuous) lymphatics can be visualized by imaging.
Scrotal lymphangiectasia is detectable by ultrasound in men with W. bancrofti infection.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Acute Adenolymphangitis (ADL) and Dermatolymphangioadenitis (DLA)
items.content: ADL presents with high fever, lymphatic inflammation, and transient local edema, while DLA includes high fever, chills, myalgias, headache, and demarcated inflammatory plaques.
keyPoints: ADL is characterized by high fever, lymphangitis, lymphadenitis, and transient local edema.
DLA presents with high fever, chills, myalgias, headache, and edematous inflammatory plaques.
Entry lesions in DLA are common, especially in the interdigital area.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Timing and Techniques for Microfilariae Detection in Lymphatic Filariasis
items.content: A definitive diagnosis of filarial infections requires the detection of parasites, which can be challenging due to their localization. Blood collection timing is crucial based on microfilariae periodicity in endemic regions. Enhanced sensitivity techniques include using polycarbonate cylindrical-pore filters (3 µm) or Knott's concentration technique with centrifugation and 2% formalin.
keyPoints: Detection of adult worms is difficult due to their localization in lymphatic vessels or nodes.
Microfilariae can be found in blood, hydrocele fluid, or other body fluids.
Use polycarbonate cylindrical-pore filters (3 µm) or Knott's concentration technique for greater sensitivity.
Timing of blood collection is critical based on microfilariae periodicity.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Diagnostic Methods and Challenges in Filarial Infections
items.content: Diagnosis can be challenging due to the absence of microfilaremia in some infected individuals. Circulating antigen assays for W. bancrofti have high sensitivity (93–100%) and specificity (approaching 100%), but false positives may occur with Loa loa infection. PCR-based DNA assays are highly sensitive, potentially surpassing parasitologic methods. Ultrasound can identify motile adult worms in lymphatics, known as the filarial dance sign.
keyPoints: Circulating antigen assays for W. bancrofti have sensitivities of 93–100% and specificities approaching 100%.
False positives can occur with Loa loa infection.
PCR-based DNA assays for W. bancrofti and B. malayi are highly sensitive.
Ultrasound may reveal motile adult worms in lymphatics, known as the filarial dance sign.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Treatment Approaches Based on Infection Status in Lymphatic Filariasis
items.content: Treatment strategies for lymphatic filariasis are tailored based on infection status, utilizing tools like ultrasound and antigen assays. DEC remains the primary treatment for active infections, while albendazole and doxycycline also show efficacy.
keyPoints: DEC (6 mg/kg daily for 12 days) is preferred for treating active lymphatic filariasis.
Albendazole (400 mg twice daily for 21 days) demonstrates macrofilaricidal efficacy.
A 4- to 6-week course of doxycycline targets Wolbachia, showing significant macrofilaricidal activity.
Combination regimens like DEC/albendazole and albendazole/ivermectin are used for eradication efforts in Africa and elsewhere.
Early treatment is recommended for asymptomatic individuals with microfilaremia to prevent lymphatic damage.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · Lymphatic Filariasis

items.title: Management of Chronic Manifestations and Side Effects in Lymphatic Filariasis
items.content: Chronic manifestations are managed through hygiene, prevention of secondary infections, and physiotherapy. Drug treatment is reserved for active infection evidence, with doxycycline sometimes improving lymphedema irrespective of activity.
keyPoints: Supportive treatments include antipyretics, analgesics, and antibiotics if bacterial infection is likely.
Chronic management emphasizes hygiene, secondary infection prevention, and physiotherapy (complex decongestive physiotherapy).
Hydroceles can be managed surgically.
Doxycycline may improve filarial lymphedema regardless of disease activity in some settings.
DEC side effects include fever, chills, arthralgias, headaches, nausea, and vomiting, related to microfilariae levels.
Ivermectin shares a similar side effect profile with DEC; caution is needed in patients with high L. loa microfilaremia.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · Lymphatic Filariasis

items.title: Mass Drug Administration for Lymphatic Filariasis Elimination
items.content: The Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis, initiated by the WHO in 1997, employs mass drug administration (MDA) as a strategy to eliminate lymphatic filariasis. This involves annual distribution of specific antifilarial medications: DEC plus albendazole in non-African regions and albendazole plus ivermectin in Africa. The goal is to suppress microfilaremia sustainably, thereby interrupting transmission.
keyPoints: Mass drug administration (MDA) involves annual distribution of antifilarial medications.
Non-African regions use DEC plus albendazole; African regions use albendazole plus ivermectin.
>935 million persons in 68 countries participated as of late 2022.
Elimination achieved in some areas with collateral benefits like treatment of other conditions.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PREVENTION AND CONTROL | TROPICAL PULMONARY EOSINOPHILIA | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Clinical Features and Diagnosis of Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia
items.content: Tropical pulmonary eosinophilia (TPE) is characterized by symptoms such as nocturnal paroxysmal cough, wheezing, weight loss, low-grade fever, lymphadenopathy, and pronounced blood eosinophilia (>3000 eosinophils/μL). Diagnostic imaging may show increased bronchovascular markings or mottled opacities. Pulmonary function tests often reveal restrictive abnormalities, with obstructive defects in half of the cases. Elevated total serum IgE levels (4–40 KIU/mL) and antifilarial antibody levels are also characteristic.
keyPoints: Symptoms include nocturnal cough, wheezing, weight loss, low-grade fever, lymphadenopathy, and eosinophilia (>3000 eosinophils/μL).
Chest x-rays or CT scans may show increased bronchovascular markings or mottled opacities.
Pulmonary function tests reveal restrictive abnormalities in most cases and obstructive defects in half.
Elevated total serum IgE levels (4–40 KIU/mL) and antifilarial antibody levels are characteristic.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PREVENTION AND CONTROL | TROPICAL PULMONARY EOSINOPHILIA | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.subtopic: PATHOLOGY | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia
items.title: Understanding the Pathology of TPE: Mechanisms and Consequences
items.content: Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia (TPE) involves rapid clearance of microfilariae and parasite antigens by the lungs, leading to allergic and inflammatory reactions. These reactions can cause symptoms due to cytotoxic proinflammatory eosinophil granule proteins. Without treatment, interstitial fibrosis may result in progressive pulmonary damage.
keyPoints: Microfilariae are rapidly cleared from the bloodstream by the lungs.
Clinical symptoms arise from allergic and inflammatory reactions.
Cytotoxic proinflammatory eosinophil granule proteins contribute to pathology.
Untreated cases can lead to interstitial fibrosis and progressive pulmonary damage.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PATHOLOGY | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of TPE: Key Considerations
items.content: Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia must be differentiated from other conditions such as asthma, Löffler's syndrome, allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis, eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Churg-Strauss syndrome), systemic vasculitides like periarteritis nodosa, chronic eosinophilic pneumonia, and hypereosinophilic syndromes.
keyPoints: Differential diagnosis includes asthma, Löffler's syndrome, allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis.
Consider eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Churg-Strauss syndrome).
Systemic vasculitides like periarteritis nodosa are important differentials.
Other considerations include chronic eosinophilic pneumonia and hypereosinophilic syndromes.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PATHOLOGY | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Pathogenesis of Onchocerciasis and Its Ocular Complications
items.content: Onchocerciasis is primarily caused by microfilariae, leading to chronic inflammatory changes in the skin, eyes, and lymph nodes. In the eye, neovascularization and corneal scarring result in blindness due to corneal opacities. The pathogenesis of most manifestations remains unclear.
keyPoints: Microfilariae cause damage, not adult parasites.
Neovascularization and corneal scarring lead to blindness.
Pathogenesis of many manifestations is still unclear.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Complications of Onchocerciasis
items.content: Onchocerciasis presents with pruritus, rash, and onchocercomata. Visual impairment is a serious complication, often due to sclerosing keratitis in 1–5% of cases. Lymphadenopathy is common, particularly in the inguinal and femoral areas. Other manifestations include cachexia, Nakalanga dwarfism, and an association with epilepsy.
keyPoints: Pruritus and rash are common; onchocercomata contain adult worms.
Sclerosing keratitis causes blindness in 1–5% of cases.
Lymphadenopathy is prevalent in inguinal/femoral areas.
Cachexia, Nakalanga dwarfism, and epilepsy association noted.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: How to Diagnose Onchocerciasis: Skin Snip vs. PCR
items.content: Definitive diagnosis of onchocerciasis relies on detecting microfilariae in a skin snip or adult worms in excised nodules. A 1-3 mm skin biopsy is incubated for 2-4 hours to allow emergent microfilariae to be observed under low-power microscopy, with PCR as an alternative detection method.
keyPoints: Skin biopsies are taken using a corneal-scleral punch or needle tip and excised with a sterile scalpel blade.
Biopsy tissue is incubated for 2-4 hours (or overnight in light infections) to allow microfilariae emergence.
Microfilariae can be detected by low-power microscopy or PCR after incubation.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Onchocerciasis

items.title: Eosinophilia and IgE in Onchocerciasis: Non-specific Markers
items.content: While eosinophilia and elevated serum IgE levels are common in onchocerciasis, they are not diagnostic due to their presence in many parasitic infections. Immunoassays detecting antibodies to Onchocerca-specific antigens offer more specific diagnosis.
keyPoints: Eosinophilia and elevated serum IgE levels are non-specific markers for onchocerciasis.
Immunoassays for Onchocerca-specific antigens provide a more definitive diagnostic approach.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Onchocerciasis

items.title: Ivermectin in Onchocerciasis Control
items.content: Community-based administration of ivermectin every 6–12 months is effective in interrupting onchocerciasis transmission, especially when combined with vector control. This strategy has led to the elimination of infection in most of Latin America and reduced prevalence in Africa.
keyPoints: Ivermectin administered every 6–12 months
Effective in conjunction with vector control
Eliminated onchocerciasis in most of Latin America
Reduced prevalence in many endemic foci in Africa
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · ☐ PREVENTION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | PATHOLOGY

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Epidemiology of Loiasis
items.content: Loiasis, caused by L. loa, presents differently in natives versus non-natives. Natives often have asymptomatic infections with microfilaremia, while non-natives experience more allergic symptoms, frequent Calabar swellings, less common microfilaremia, and characteristic eosinophilia and antifilarial antibodies.
keyPoints: L. loa causes loiasis
Adult parasites: females 50–70 mm long, males 25–35 mm long
Microfilariae circulate with diurnal peak between 10:00 A.M. and 2:00 P.M.
Natives often asymptomatic with microfilaremia
Non-natives experience allergic symptoms, frequent Calabar swellings
Eosinophilia and increased antifilarial antibodies in non-natives
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · ☐ PREVENTION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | PATHOLOGY

items.title: Diagnosis of Loiasis: Key Considerations and Approaches
items.content: Loiasis diagnosis involves detecting microfilariae in peripheral blood or isolating adult worms from the eye or subcutaneous biopsy. PCR assays for L. loa DNA are highly sensitive and specific, along with some serologic techniques. Diagnosis often relies on clinical presentation, history, eosinophilia, and antifilarial antibodies, especially in travelers.
keyPoints: Detection of microfilariae or isolation of adult worms is definitive.
PCR assays for L. loa DNA are highly sensitive and specific.
Diagnosis often based on clinical presentation, history, eosinophilia, and antifilarial antibodies.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Loiasis | STREPTOCERCIASIS

items.title: Treatment Considerations for Loiasis: Managing Microfilaremia and Adverse Effects
items.content: DEC (8–10 mg/kg per day) is effective against both adult and microfilarial forms of L. loa, but multiple courses may be needed. In heavy microfilaremia, apheresis and glucocorticoids are used initially to manage inflammatory reactions. Albendazole or ivermectin reduces microfilarial loads; however, ivermectin is contraindicated in patients with >30,000 microfilariae/mL due to severe adverse events.
keyPoints: DEC (8–10 mg/kg per day) for 21 days effective against L. loa forms.
Apheresis and glucocorticoids used initially in heavy microfilaremia.
Ivermectin contraindicated with >30,000 microfilariae/mL due to risk of severe adverse events.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Loiasis | STREPTOCERCIASIS

items.subtopic: MANSONELLA PERSTANS INFECTION | MANSONELA OZZARDI INFECTION | ZOONOTIC FILARIAL INFECTIONS
items.title: Effective Treatment for Mansonella perstans Infection
items.content: Mansonella perstans infection is treated effectively with doxycycline due to the presence of a Wolbachia endosymbiont. The recommended regimen involves 200 mg twice daily for 6 weeks.
keyPoints: Doxycycline (200 mg twice a day) for 6 weeks is effective against M. perstans infection.
Presence of Wolbachia endosymbiont in M. perstans makes doxycycline treatment viable.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · MANSONELLA PERSTANS INFECTION | MANSONELLA OZZARDI INFECTION | ZOONOTIC FILARIAL INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: MANSONELLA PERSTANS INFECTION | MANSONELA OZZARDI INFECTION | ZOONOTIC FILARIAL INFECTIONS
items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Mansonella ozzardi Infection
items.content: Mansonella ozzardi infection can manifest with symptoms such as headache, articular pain, fever, pulmonary symptoms, adenopathy, hepatomegaly, pruritus, and eosinophilia. Diagnosis is confirmed by detecting microfilariae in peripheral blood.
keyPoints: Symptoms of M. ozzardi infection include headache, articular pain, fever, pulmonary symptoms, adenopathy, hepatomegaly, pruritus, and eosinophilia.
Diagnosis involves detection of microfilariae in peripheral blood.
Ivermectin is effective for treating M. ozzardi infection.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · MANSONELLA PERSTANS INFECTION | MANSONELLA OZZARDI INFECTION | ZOONOTIC FILARIAL INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Dracunculiasis | ☐ PREVENTION
items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology and Transmission of Dracunculiasis
items.content: Dracunculiasis, caused by Dracunculus medinensis, has seen a dramatic decline due to global eradication efforts. However, recent years have shown fluctuations in case numbers, with 13 human cases reported across four African countries at the end of 2022. The persistence of infection in animal reservoirs like dogs and cats poses challenges to eradication.
keyPoints: Incidence has declined due to global eradication efforts.
Recent reports: 13 human cases by the end of 2022 across Chad (6), South Sudan (5), Ethiopia (1), Central African Republic (1).
Animal reservoirs (dogs and cats) present a challenge for eradication.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Dracunculiasis | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Dracunculiasis | ☐ PREVENTION
items.title: Clinical Features and Diagnosis of Dracunculiasis
items.content: Dracunculiasis typically presents with few symptoms until the blister forms, at which point fever, periorbital edema, wheezing, and urticaria may occur. The emergence of the worm from a ruptured blister is associated with local pain and swelling. Diagnosis relies on observing these clinical features as the adult worm emerges.
keyPoints: Symptoms appear just before blister formation: fever, periorbital edema, wheezing, urticaria.
Emergence of the worm causes local pain and swelling.
Diagnosis is based on clinical observation during worm emergence.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Dracunculiasis | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Filarial and Related Infections | LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS
items.question: Which filarial species are responsible for most symptomatic infections in humans?
options: Wuchereria bancrofti, Brugia malayi, Onchocerca volvulus, Loa loa
Onchocerca volvulus, Loa loa, Mansonella perstans, Wuchereria bancrofti
Brugia timori, Mansonella ozzardi, Dirofilaria immitis, Wuchereria bancrofti
Loa loa, Brugia malayi, Onchocerca volvulus, Dirofilaria repens
items.explanation: Wuchereria bancrofti, Brugia malayi, Onchocerca volvulus, and Loa loa are responsible for most symptomatic filarial infections.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · Filarial and Related Infections | LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS

items.subtopic: Filarial and Related Infections | LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS
items.question: How long can adult worms of lymphatic filariasis live in the human body?
items.explanation: Adult worms of lymphatic filariasis can reside in the human body for more than two decades.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · Filarial and Related Infections | LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS

items.subtopic: Filarial and Related Infections | LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS
items.question: What is a target for antifilarial chemotherapy due to its presence in all stages of certain filarial species?
items.explanation: The intracellular bacterial endosymbiont Wolbachia is a target for antifilarial chemotherapy.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · Filarial and Related Infections | LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS

items.question: Which mosquito species are the natural vectors for W. bancrofti in urban settings?
items.explanation: Natural vectors for W. bancrofti in urban settings are Culex mosquitoes.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOLOGY

items.question: In which areas is the subperiodic form of Brugian filariasis due to B. malayi found?
items.explanation: The subperiodic form of B. malayi is found in forests.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOLOGY

items.question: What are the principal pathologic changes caused by lymphatic filariasis?
items.explanation: The principal pathologic changes of lymphatic filariasis result from inflammatory damage to the lymphatics.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is a common clinical feature in individuals with W. bancrofti or B. malayi microfilaremia?
items.explanation: Most individuals with W. bancrofti or B. malayi microfilaremia have subclinical disease including microscopic hematuria and/or proteinuria.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of acute adenolymphangitis (ADL) in filarial infections?
options: Retrograde lymphatic inflammation extending peripherally from the draining lymph node
Persistent low-grade fever without other symptoms
items.explanation: ADL is characterized by retrograde lymphangitis, which extends peripherally from the lymph node draining the area where adult parasites reside.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: Which condition can develop as a result of prolonged exposure to infected mosquitoes in travelers entering an endemic region?
items.explanation: Prolonged exposure to infected mosquitoes can lead to permanent lymphatic inflammation and obstruction.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: Which method is used to increase the sensitivity of detecting microfilariae in body fluids?
options: Passage of fluid through a polycarbonate cylindrical-pore filter
items.explanation: The passage describes using a polycarbonate cylindrical-pore filter to increase sensitivity in detecting microfilariae.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which diagnostic method has sensitivities equivalent to or greater than parasitologic methods for W. bancrofti and B. malayi?
items.explanation: The passage states that PCR-based assays have sensitivities equivalent to or greater than parasitologic methods.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the 'filarial dance sign' indicative of in lymphatic filariasis diagnosis?
options: Motile adult worms within dilated lymphatics
Cross-reactivity between filarial and other helminth antigens
items.explanation: 'Filarial dance sign' refers to the distinctive movement pattern of live adult worms within dilated lymphatics.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which drug combination has been shown to sustain microfilarial clearance for at least 2 years in lymphatic filariasis?
items.explanation: A regimen using single doses of albendazole, DEC, and ivermectin has been shown to sustain microfilarial clearance for at least 2 years.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · Lymphatic Filariasis

items.question: What is the recommended treatment approach for asymptomatic persons with W. bancrofti or B. malayi microfilaremia?
options: No treatment, as they are asymptomatic
Early treatment to prevent further lymphatic damage
Only supportive treatment like antipyretics and analgesics
items.explanation: Early treatment is recommended for asymptomatic persons with microfilaremia to prevent further lymphatic damage.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · Lymphatic Filariasis

items.question: What side effects are associated with DEC treatment in infected individuals?
options: Fever, chills, arthralgias, headaches, nausea, and vomiting
items.explanation: DEC treatment can cause fever, chills, arthralgias, headaches, nausea, and vomiting.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · Lymphatic Filariasis

items.subtopic: PREVENTION AND CONTROL | TROPICAL PULMONARY EOSINOPHILIA | CLINICAL FEATURES
items.question: Which combination of drugs is used for mass drug administration (MDA) in non-African regions to eliminate lymphatic filariasis?
items.explanation: In non-African regions, the combination of DEC plus albendazole is used for MDA to eliminate lymphatic filariasis.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PREVENTION AND CONTROL | TROPICAL PULMONARY EOSINOPHILIA | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.subtopic: PREVENTION AND CONTROL | TROPICAL PULMONARY EOSINOPHILIA | CLINICAL FEATURES
items.question: What is a key clinical feature of tropical pulmonary eosinophilia (TPE) related to microfilariae?
items.explanation: A key clinical feature of TPE is nocturnal paroxysmal cough and wheezing, likely due to the nocturnal periodicity of microfilariae.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PREVENTION AND CONTROL | TROPICAL PULMONARY EOSINOPHILIA | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.subtopic: PREVENTION AND CONTROL | TROPICAL PULMONARY EOSINOPHILIA | CLINICAL FEATURES
items.question: What is a common radiographic finding in tropical pulmonary eosinophilia (TPE)?
options: Normal chest x-rays or CT scans
items.explanation: Chest x-rays or CT scans in TPE generally show increased bronchovascular markings.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PREVENTION AND CONTROL | TROPICAL PULMONARY EOSINOPHILIA | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.subtopic: PATHOLOGY | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
items.question: What is a common pathological finding in the lungs of patients with Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia (TPE)?
items.explanation: A prominent, eosinophil-enriched, intra-alveolar infiltrate is common in TPE.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PATHOLOGY | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following conditions must be differentiated from Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia (TPE)?
items.explanation: TPE must be distinguished from asthma, among other conditions.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PATHOLOGY | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: PATHOLOGY | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
items.question: What is the typical duration for symptoms to resolve after initiating therapy for TPE?
items.explanation: Symptoms usually resolve within 3–7 days after the initiation of therapy for TPE.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PATHOLOGY | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following best describes the primary cause of pathology in onchocerciasis?
options: Damage is caused by adult parasites.
Microfilariae elicit damage, not adult parasites.
Pathology is primarily due to bacterial infection.
The adult worm causes direct tissue necrosis.
items.explanation: In onchocerciasis, the pathology is caused by microfilariae rather than adult parasites.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: What is a common ocular manifestation of onchocerciasis that can lead to blindness?
items.explanation: Sclerosing keratitis is the leading cause of blindness in onchocerciasis.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: Where are onchocercomata most commonly found?
options: In the palms and soles of the feet.
On bony prominences like the coccyx and sacrum.
items.explanation: Onchocercomata are most common over bony prominences such as the coccyx and sacrum.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: Which method is commonly used to detect microfilariae in the diagnosis of onchocerciasis?
options: Detection of adult worms in blood samples
Skin snips obtained with a corneal-scleral punch or needle lift
items.explanation: Microfilariae are commonly detected using skin snips.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Onchocerciasis

items.question: What is the first-line agent for the treatment of onchocerciasis?
items.explanation: Ivermectin is the first-line agent for treating onchocerciasis.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Onchocerciasis

items.question: Why are eosinophilia and elevated serum IgE levels not diagnostic of onchocerciasis?
options: They are seen in many parasitic infections.
They are specific to viral infections.
items.explanation: Eosinophilia and elevated serum IgE levels are common in many parasitic infections, not just onchocerciasis.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Onchocerciasis

items.subtopic: ☐ PREVENTION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | PATHOLOGY
items.question: Which of the following is a key strategy used to interrupt transmission of onchocerciasis in endemic areas?
options: Community-based administration of ivermectin every 6–12 months
Use of bed nets treated with long-lasting insecticides
items.explanation: Community-based administration of ivermectin is used to interrupt transmission in endemic areas.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · ☐ PREVENTION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: ☐ PREVENTION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | PATHOLOGY
items.question: What is the diurnal periodicity peak time for microfilariae of L. loa in the blood?
items.explanation: Microfilariae of L. loa peak between 10:00 A.M. and 2:00 P.M.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · ☐ PREVENTION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: ☐ PREVENTION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | PATHOLOGY
items.question: What is the typical clinical manifestation of loiasis in non-residents of endemic areas?
options: Episodic Calabar swellings and allergic symptoms
items.explanation: Non-residents often experience episodic Calabar swelling and allergic symptoms.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · ☐ PREVENTION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | PATHOLOGY

items.question: Which method is NOT a definitive diagnostic approach for loiasis?
options: Detection of microfilariae in peripheral blood
Isolation of adult worm from the eye
PCR-based assays for L. loa DNA detection
items.explanation: Definitive diagnosis requires microfilariae detection or isolation of the adult worm, not just elevated antibody levels.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Loiasis | STREPTOCERCIASIS

items.question: What is a potential risk when treating heavily infected loiasis patients with ivermectin?
options: Development of resistance to the drug
Severe allergic reactions including encephalopathy and death
Improvement in pruritus and pigmentation changes
items.explanation: Ivermectin is contraindicated for patients with >30,000 microfilariae/mL due to severe adverse events.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Loiasis | STREPTOCERCIASIS

items.question: Which treatment regimen is effective as a prophylactic measure against loiasis?
options: Ivermectin at a single dose of 150 $ \mu $g/kg
DEC (8–10 mg/kg per day) administered orally for 21 days
items.explanation: DEC at 300 mg weekly is an effective prophylactic regimen for loiasis.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Loiasis | STREPTOCERCIASIS

items.subtopic: MANSONELLA PERSTANS INFECTION | MANSONELLA OZZARDI INFECTION | ZOONOTIC FILARIAL INFECTIONS
items.question: Where do adult M. perstans worms typically reside in the human body?
options: In serous cavities such as pericardial, pleural, and peritoneal spaces
items.explanation: Adult M. perstans worms reside in serous cavities—pericardial, pleural, and peritoneal—as well as in mesentery and perirenal tissues.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · MANSONELLA PERSTANS INFECTION | MANSONELLA OZZARDI INFECTION | ZOONOTIC FILARIAL INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: MANSONELLA PERSTANS INFECTION | MANSONELLA OZZARDI INFECTION | ZOONOTIC FILARIAL INFECTIONS
items.question: What is the first effective treatment established for M. perstans infection?
items.explanation: With the identification of a Wolbachia endosymbiont in M. perstans, doxycycline (200 mg twice a day) for 6 weeks has been established as the first effective treatment.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · MANSONELLA PERSTANS INFECTION | MANSONELLA OZZARDI INFECTION | ZOONOTIC FILARIAL INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: MANSONELLA PERSTANS INFECTION | MANSONELLA OZZARDI INFECTION | ZOONOTIC FILARIAL INFECTIONS
items.question: Which of the following is a common clinical manifestation of M. ozzardi infection?
items.explanation: M. ozzardi infection can cause headache, articular pain, fever, pulmonary symptoms, adenopathy, hepatomegaly, pruritus, and eosinophilia.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · MANSONELLA PERSTANS INFECTION | MANSONELLA OZZARDI INFECTION | ZOONOTIC FILARIAL INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Dracunculiasis | ☐ PREVENTION
items.question: Which of the following is a major challenge to ongoing eradication efforts for dracunculiasis?
options: Persistence of infection in animal reservoirs such as dogs and cats
High mutation rate of Dracunculus medinensis
items.explanation: Low-level persistence of infection in animal reservoirs is a major challenge to ongoing eradication efforts.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Dracunculiasis | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Dracunculiasis | ☐ PREVENTION
items.question: How do humans typically acquire dracunculiasis?
options: Through direct contact with infected animals
By ingesting water containing infective larvae derived from Cyclops
Via airborne transmission of the larvae
items.explanation: Humans acquire Dracunculus medinensis by ingesting water containing infective larvae derived from Cyclops.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Dracunculiasis | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Dracunculiasis | ☐ PREVENTION
items.question: What is the primary method of preventing dracunculiasis?
items.explanation: Prevention depends on the provision of safe drinking water.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Dracunculiasis | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Filarial and Related Infections | LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS
items.statement: Lymphatic filariasis can be caused by Brugia timori.
items.explanation: Brugia timori is one of the causative agents of lymphatic filariasis.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · Filarial and Related Infections | LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS

items.subtopic: Filarial and Related Infections | LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS
items.statement: Microfilariae survive for more than 5 years.
items.explanation: Microfilariae survive for 3–36 months, not more than 5 years.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · Filarial and Related Infections | LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS

items.statement: Humans are not the only definitive host for W. bancrofti.
items.explanation: Humans are the only definitive host for W. bancrofti.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOLOGY

items.statement: The death of a filarial worm leads to enhanced granulomatous reactions and fibrosis.
items.explanation: The death of the worm leads to enhanced granulomatous reactions and fibrosis.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOLOGY

items.statement: In bancroftian filariasis, hydroceles may develop in advanced stages.
items.explanation: Hydroceles can develop in advanced stages of bancroftian filariasis.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: Chyluria is a permanent and constant condition in individuals with lymphatic filariasis.
items.explanation: Chyluria is usually intermittent and most prominent in the morning, not a permanent or constant condition.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: Seropositivity for W. bancrofti and B. malayi-specific antigens confirms active infection.
items.explanation: The passage notes that seropositivity cannot be equated with active infection as residents can become sensitized without having patent infections.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Radionuclide lymphoscintigraphic imaging is the primary diagnostic tool for suspected filarial infection.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that lymphoscintigraphy is mainly a research tool and not the primary diagnostic method.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Doxycycline is used in lymphatic filariasis to target the intracellular Wolbachia.
items.explanation: Doxycycline targets the intracellular Wolbachia, contributing to its macrofilaricidal activity.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · Lymphatic Filariasis

items.statement: Albendazole is associated with severe side effects when used in single-dose regimens for lymphatic filariasis.
items.explanation: Albendazole, when used in single-dose regimens, is associated with relatively few side effects.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · Lymphatic Filariasis

items.subtopic: PREVENTION AND CONTROL | TROPICAL PULMONARY EOSINOPHILIA | CLINICAL FEATURES
items.statement: The Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis was created by the World Health Organization in 1997.
items.explanation: The WHO established the Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis in 1997.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PREVENTION AND CONTROL | TROPICAL PULMONARY EOSINOPHILIA | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.subtopic: PREVENTION AND CONTROL | TROPICAL PULMONARY EOSINOPHILIA | CLINICAL FEATURES
items.statement: Tropical pulmonary eosinophilia is most commonly reported from North America.
items.explanation: Most cases of tropical pulmonary eosinophilia are reported from India, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Brazil, Guyana, and Southeast Asia.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PREVENTION AND CONTROL | TROPICAL PULMONARY EOSINOPHILIA | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.subtopic: PATHOLOGY | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
items.statement: Onchocerciasis is primarily found in the equatorial region of Africa.
items.explanation: The majority of individuals infected with O. volvulus live in the equatorial region of Africa.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PATHOLOGY | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: PATHOLOGY | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY
items.statement: The life span of adult O. volvulus can be up to 5 years.
items.explanation: The life span of adults can be as long as 18 years, with an average of ~9 years.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PATHOLOGY | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Tropical Pulmonary Eosinophilia | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Punctate keratitis in onchocerciasis is a permanent condition.
items.explanation: Punctate keratitis, or 'snowflake' opacities, often resolves without complications.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: Nodding syndrome is a recognized association with onchocerciasis.
items.explanation: There is an association between onchocerciasis and epilepsy, including nodding syndrome.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: Ivermectin is contraindicated in areas co-endemic for O. volvulus and L. loa.
items.explanation: Ivermectin can cause severe posttreatment encephalopathy in such areas.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Onchocerciasis

items.statement: Doxycycline is a microfilaricidal agent.
items.explanation: Doxycycline is macrofilaristatic, not microfilaricidal.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Onchocerciasis

items.subtopic: ☐ PREVENTION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | PATHOLOGY
items.statement: No drug has proved useful for prophylaxis of O. volvulus infection.
items.explanation: There is no effective prophylactic drug for O. volvulus.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · ☐ PREVENTION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: ☐ PREVENTION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | PATHOLOGY
items.statement: Loiasis is always symptomatic in the indigenous population.
items.explanation: In natives, loiasis can often be asymptomatic with microfilaremia.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · ☐ PREVENTION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | PATHOLOGY

items.statement: Albendazole and ivermectin are approved by the U.S. FDA for reducing microfilarial loads in loiasis.
items.explanation: Neither albendazole nor ivermectin is approved by the U.S. FDA for this purpose.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Loiasis | STREPTOCERCIASIS

items.statement: Mansonella streptocerca can be diagnosed by detection of microfilariae in skin snips.
items.explanation: The diagnosis of streptocerciasis is made by detecting characteristic microfilariae in skin snips.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Loiasis | STREPTOCERCIASIS

items.subtopic: MANSONELLA PERSTANS INFECTION | MANSONELLA OZZARDI INFECTION | ZOONOTIC FILARIAL INFECTIONS
items.statement: Microfilariae of M. perstans circulate in the blood with periodicity.
items.explanation: M. perstans microfilariae circulate in the blood without periodicity.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · MANSONELLA PERSTANS INFECTION | MANSONELLA OZZARDI INFECTION | ZOONOTIC FILARIAL INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: MANSONELLA PERSTANS INFECTION | MANSONELLA OZZARDI INFECTION | ZOONOTIC FILARIAL INFECTIONS
items.statement: Humans are a normal host for Dirofilaria immitis, allowing full development of the parasite.
items.explanation: Humans are an abnormal host for Dirofilaria immitis; the parasites never develop fully.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · MANSONELLA PERSTANS INFECTION | MANSONELLA OZZARDI INFECTION | ZOONOTIC FILARIAL INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Dracunculiasis | ☐ PREVENTION
items.statement: The adult male Dracunculus medinensis survives and mates with the female worm.
items.explanation: The adult male probably dies after mating.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Dracunculiasis | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Dracunculiasis | ☐ PREVENTION
items.statement: Dracunculiasis can be diagnosed based on clinical findings when the adult worm emerges.
items.explanation: Diagnosis is based on findings developing with the emergence of the adult worm.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSIS | Dracunculiasis | ☐ PREVENTION

items.assertion: Treatment with DEC is recommended for microfilaria-negative carriers of adult worms.
items.reason: The presence of adult worms indicates lymphatic disease, necessitating treatment to prevent further complications.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why DEC is recommended for microfilaria-negative carriers.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · Lymphatic Filariasis

items.assertion: Onchocercomata are firm and not tender.
items.reason: They contain the adult worm surrounded by fibrous tissue.
items.explanation: The presence of the adult worm in a fibrous capsule explains why onchocercomata are firm and not tender.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES

items.subtopic: ☐ PREVENTION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | PATHOLOGY
items.assertion: Calabar swellings in loiasis are thought to result from a hypersensitivity reaction.
items.reason: This is due to adult worm antigens triggering the immune response.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Filarial and Related Infections · ☐ PREVENTION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL FEATURES | PATHOLOGY
